environment.machine: Mac16,7 (Apple Silicon), 14 cores, 24 GB RAM, macOS Darwin 25.2.0
environment.benchJvm: Eclipse Temurin 17.0.6 (separate JVM per engine via Gradle JavaExec)

corpora.apache/camel@camel-4.4.0: 548 non-src reactor poms; tiers 50/150/400 via Corpus.selectSubtree (all 47 pom-packaging scaffolding modules + path-sorted artifact leaves; caps are strict supersets)

methodology.warm: median of 7 measured iterations after 2 warm-up full-reactor builds; shared caches (OLD: InMemoryMavenPomCache; NEW: RepositoryCache+ModelCache), fresh session/ctx per iteration; requests(last iter)=0 for OLD, ~6 residual failed-lookup probes/iter for camel NEW (fresh session re-probes non-existent poms)
methodology.loop: one leaf module re-resolved x50 against warm caches (3 warm-up); median per-iteration; same leaf pinned for both engines per tier (loopLeaf field)
methodology.cold: apache/maven only (single live-network shot); skipped for camel tiers (bench.skipCold) - warm scaling is the question, live-network over thousands of poms is disproportionate
methodology.modelCacheClones: CountingModelCache counts cache hits per tag during ONE additional warm full-reactor build; Maven 3.9 DefaultModelBuilder deep-clones on every RAW hit (Model.clone) and IMPORT hit (DependencyManagement.clone), so hits == clones

engines.old: released rewrite-maven resolution (MavenParser, all four scopes)
engines.new_n1: raw pipeline, one verbose collect (test scope) per pom + effective model build
engines.new_n4: raw pipeline, four per-scope collects per pom (apples-to-apples with OLD's four-scope work)

notes: Machine suspended (laptop lid) twice during the session; spread audit over all recorded tiers showed no suspend artifacts in measured phases (all warm max/min <= 1.17). camel OLD-400 was suspended during its unmeasured network warm-up; killed and re-run fresh to completion.

RE-MEASURED: camel OLD tier 150. The first OLD-150 measurement (warm 4756 ms, loop 9.9 ms) was invalid for two compounding reasons: (a) methodology inconsistency - it alone pointed the local repository at the resolver-populated repo (a warm-up shortcut experiment); rewrite-maven treats the local repo as a file:// repository, so every warm iteration paid thousands of local file reads that OLD-50/OLD-400 (empty local repo) did not - the persistent 2x inflation of the 5 ms loop metric on the same leaf (9.9 vs 4.9 ms) is the mechanical fingerprint; (b) it was recorded near a machine-suspend window. Re-run under the standard empty-local-repo methodology: warm 2016.4 ms, loop 4.731 ms - restoring OLD tier monotonicity (1177 -> 2016 -> 4016 ms). Recorded numbers are from the re-run; all OLD camel tiers are methodologically identical (empty local repo, unmeasured network warm-up, in-memory pom cache serving measured iterations at requests=0).

Engine pairing per tier is guaranteed by construction: both engines run the same Corpus.selectSubtree from the shared common source set on the same corpus path; every RESULT line records modules= and both engines' LOOP lines record the same leaf (asserted at JSON-assembly time). NEW full-548 has no OLD counterpart by design (see next note).
camel OLD tiers 150 and 400 each had 2 parse failures (poms yielding no resolution result; NEW built all models at every tier); counts reported as measured.

camel tier 50 loop leaf is components/camel-debezium/camel-debezium-common (deterministic deepest-path fallback; tier 50 = 47 scaffolding poms + 3 archetypes, no jar component in tier). Tiers >=150 pin components/camel-activemq. Loop comparisons are valid within a tier, not across camel tiers 50->150.

OLD full-548 tier skipped: OLD pays a 20-40 min unmeasured serial network warm-up per tier (rewrite-maven does not persist poms to a local repo) and the OLD curve already has 4 points; NEW full-548 included (the ModelCache scaling deliverable).

graphNodes counts differ by design between engines: OLD = winner-only resolved deps summed over 4 scopes; NEW-N1 = verbose graph (retained conflict losers), one scope; NEW-N4 = verbose nodes summed across 4 overlapping scope subgraphs.
